data.title: Ero Copper downgraded at Goldman following recent rally
data.description:
Ero Copper (ERO) down 6.5% in Tuesday's trading as Goldman Sachs downgraded shares to Neutral from Buy with a $31 price target after the stock more than doubled, a move that can be attributed mostly to strong commodities prices and production growth, despite slower thanexpected operational momentum.

Goldman analyst Marcio Farid now sees Ero's (ERO) risk/reward as less attractive due to limited copper price upside, some remaining operational uncertainties, and a less favorable valuation, adding that momentum related to the Furnas project is a key to watch for the potential to become more constructive.

Ero (ERO) has underperformed its guidance for the past three years, Farid said, believing FY 2026 guidance still requires additional operational improvement to be met, and he thinks it is unlikely that the market will pay for a re-rate before the company delivers.

The Goldman Sachs commodities team expects copper prices to come in flat to marginally down in 2026-27, with the global copper market likely to be in surplus for this year, and it sees copper prices not being supported by fundamentals, making it more vulnerable to another move lowershould the economic outlook deteriorate and investors de-risk.

data.title: Philip Morris's white paper pushes for smoking shift, weighs on peers
data.description:
Nearly three-quarters of Americans think public health officials, policymakers, and medical professionals should do more to discourage cigarette smoking and address the misconception that non-combustible products are safer than combustible products.

“America likes to believe it has solved the problem of smoking. It hasn’t,” says a white paper authored by Philip Morris International’s (PM) U.S. business.

“The Forgotten Smoker: Modern Solutions to America’s Oldest Public Health Challenge,” calls for a renewed public health focus on adults who smoke by addressing misinformation, recognizing varying levels of health risks from both combustible and non-combustible products, expanding the availability of FDA-authorized nicotine alternatives, and preventing youth tobacco use.

The findings as well as recommendations proposed by Philip Morris (PM) are putting pressure on shares across the sector Tuesday, as many solutions proposed by Philip Morris (PM) involve smoking cessation programs, tax policies to discourage the use of the most harmful products, and investment in strategies that work for longtime, highly dependent adults who smoke. 

Shares of Philip Morris International (PM), Altria Group (MO), and British American Tobacco (BTI) are all down 2% on Tuesday. 

"If we're serious about ending cigarette smoking, the leading cause of preventable death in the U.S., we need the FDA to assess and authorize better alternatives to combustible cigarettes in a timely fashion and take a leading role in educating healthcare providers, policymakers, and the public about tobacco harm reduction and the role smoke-free products can play. This is how we will move this issue out of the shadows and solve it once and for all,” said Keagan Lenihan, Chief External Affairs Officer for Philip Morris International (PM).

While the white paper seemingly takes aim at Philip Morris’s (PM) cash cow of cigarette smoking, the company’s smoke-free products generated nearly $17B in revenue in 2025, 41.5% of the company’s total sales. And in the fourth quarter, the smoke-free business achieved a key milestone of 50% of total revenue, underscoring the company’s pivot away from traditional tobacco products to smokeless and non-combustible products. Additionally, a 1.1% decrease in cigarettes was largely offset by 10.2% growth in heated tobacco units ((HTU)).  

So rather than encourage people to quit nicotine entirely, Philip Morris’s (PM) “tobacco harm reduction” focuses on “lowering exposure to the most harmful aspects of tobacco use—especially combustion.”

The paper cites a Swedish study in which smokers steadily substituted cigarettes with smoke-free nicotine products, specifically "snus," a smokeless tobacco placed under the lip. Consequently, the study found that cigarette smoking among Swedes has declined steadily while overall nicotine use has shifted toward less harmful alternatives.  

“Ending smoking will not happen through attrition. It will happen only if the country treats cigarette smoking, and the adults who smoke, as a continuing public health priority – and designs policies that make it easier for people to move to a smoke-free life,” the company said.

data.title: MeiraGTx rises on upcoming Phase 1 readout for gene therapy
data.description:
Shares of MeiraGTx Holdings (MGTX) added ~17% to reach a 52-week high on Tuesday after the company scheduled a presentation to disclose data from an early-stage trial for its gene therapy, AAV-hAQP1.

The event, scheduled for April 16 at 8:00 a.m. ET will include three-year data from a Phase 1 trial for AAV-hAQP1, the company said in a statement on Tuesday. The one-time therapy is designed to target radiation-induced xerostomia, a long-term adverse effect of radiation therapy used in head and neck cancer.

The open-label AQUAx clinical study evaluated AAV-hAQP1 in patients with grade 2/3 moderate to severe radiation-induced xerostomia and those who remained without cancer for at least five years after radiation therapy for head and neck cancer.

The webcast to disclose its long-term data will also include a section on the drug’s commercial opportunity, MeiraGTx (MGTX) said, adding that a question-and-answer session will follow the presentation.

data.title: Goldman Sachs' private credit fund raising $750M through bond offering - report
data.description:
Goldman Sachs Group's (GS) private credit fund is said to be raising $750M through a bond sale.

Last week, the banking giant had said its private credit fund was the only non-traded business development company in the peer group that saw repurchase requests below the standard 5% quarterly cap during the first quarter.

Institutional capital helped keep redemption requests for its $15.7B private credit fund below the standard 5% quarterly cap, even as retail and some wealth management investors pulled back from the space.

Yesterday, CEO David Solomon said that his bank continues to view the private credit sector as attractive, despite some "noise."

Goldman Sachs Private Credit Corp. has tightened the pricing on the five-year notes by 30 basis points to 2.55 percentage points above Treasuries, a person familiar with the matter told Bloomberg News.

Earlier today, Blue Owl Capital (OWL) said its private credit fund, through its listed vehicle Blue Owl Capital Corp. (OBDC), raised $400M via a five-year bond offering.

The latest offering suggests a rebound in confidence in the private credit sector, the Tuesday, April 14 report noted.

The sector, including Apollo Global Management (APO), Ares Management (ARES), Blackstone (BX), Blue Owl Capital Inc. (OWL), and KKR (KKR), has reportedly received $20.8B in redemption requests during the quarter.

data.title: Citi upgrades U.S. stocks to Overweight, favors defensive positioning
data.description:
Citi Research has upgraded U.S. equities (SPY) (VOO) to Overweight from Neutral in a recent note, citing a preference for defensive positioning amid ongoing geopolitical uncertainty.

In its quarterly report, the investment bank noted that “global equities are only slightly below all-time highs after last week’s relief rally” but emphasized the need for caution. Stocks extended gains this week as investors grew optimistic about potential U.S.-Iran peace talks that could ease tensions in the Middle East.

“Against this backdrop, we adopt a Quality/Defensive tilt in our global equity strategy,” Citi analysts led by Beata Manthey wrote, pairing the U.S. upgrade with a similar overweight stance on the U.K. (EWU).

However, the firm made clear this is not a broad risk-on signal. Citi described the allocation as “more tactical in nature” because “the fluidity of geopolitical events leaves us with less visibility over the medium-term.” The bank also cautioned that its year-end price targets depend on “an eventual cessation in the US-Iran conflict.”

"While an eventual resolution should revive sentiment, a return to 'goldilocks' macro and pro-Cyclical trading dynamics may prove difficult."

Adding to the cautious tone, Citi flagged a disconnect between market pricing and earnings fundamentals. The note warned that “global equities still appear to be priced for EPS upgrades,” even as consensus forecasts of 20% earnings growth in 2026 may need to be revised lower, “especially for more Cyclical parts of the market.”

data.title: Plastics prices climb as supply disruptions ripple through petrochemical markets
data.description:
Major chemical producers including Dow Inc. (DOW) and Exxon Mobil (XOM) are raising prices for plastics in response to supply disruptions linked to the conflict involving the U.S., Israel and Iran.

Dow (DOW) has notified customers in North America that it plans to increase prices for polyethylene resins, widely used in packaging and containers, through at least May. The company outlined a 30-cent-per-pound increase for April and indicated a further 20-cent-per-pound rise could follow next month, Bloomberg News reported.

Other producers are taking similar steps. Exxon and Nova Chemicals recently moved to lift April price increases to 30 cents per pound, up from earlier plans for smaller hikes, according to separate notices.

The price moves come as petrochemical markets react to disruptions in the Middle East, where shipping through the Strait of Hormuz, a critical route for oil and liquefied natural gas, has been significantly curtailed. The fuels transported through the strait are key inputs for plastics production, and reduced availability has pushed costs higher.

Polyethylene prices in the U.S. have risen sharply since the conflict began, with some buyers reporting that prices have more than doubled. The impact has been more pronounced in Europe and Asia, which rely more heavily on supplies from the region.

Higher input costs are also affecting related industries. INX International Ink Co. said it plans to raise prices on certain ink and coating products beginning May 1, citing increased expenses for materials, energy and transportation tied to geopolitical disruptions.

Supply constraints may persist even if shipping routes reopen. Production has been affected by reduced output at oil and gas facilities, slower refinery activity and damage to some petrochemical plants.

As a result, industry executives expect elevated costs to continue for months. Dow (DOW) Chief Executive Jim Fitterling said recently that it could take much of a year for supply flows from the Persian Gulf to normalize after shipping resumes.

data.title: Private credit uncertainty will persist for some time – Carlyle CEO
data.description:
Carlyle CEO Harvey Schwartz expects uncertainty in the private credit market to persist for some time, driven by redemption requests and questions about how artificial intelligence will impact the software sector.

In a recent interview with CNBC, Schwartz acknowledged that his firm saw redemption requests of at least 15% last quarter, slightly higher than some peers, but characterized the roughly $15B in quarterly outflows as “quite orderly” in the context of global capital markets.

Despite elevated concerns, Schwartz firmly rejected the notion that private credit poses a systemic risk to the broader economy.

“Systemic is not just about the size of a marketplace. It’s about interconnectivity, and it’s about the speed at which capital can move and leverage,” he explained. “When you look through all of that, it’s pretty dispersed. It’s not as interconnected as the issues we saw back in the financial crisis.”

The Carlyle CEO identified artificial intelligence’s impact on the software industry as the central driver of current market anxiety. Software companies (IGV), (XSW), once viewed as stable sources of cash flow and revenue streams, are now facing disruption concerns that have rippled through credit markets.

Schwartz warned that the compression of valuation multiples in the software space could lead to increased defaults among private equity-backed companies.

When asked about the potential for rising defaults, he acknowledged the risk: “I think anytime you see multiple compression of this degree, then you have an issue of how exits happen, how’s value creation is determined. And I think, yeah, I think you could see an uptick in defaults just because of the multiple compression.”

Looking ahead, the CEO maintained an optimistic long-term view, describing the current period of scrutiny as a “healthy process” for a young industry.

He praised regulatory attention and the expansion into the retirement channel, noting that increased transparency and global education around private capital solutions are beneficial developments. “We’re in the early phases of a growth trajectory,” Schwartz concluded.

More on private credit

  • Goldman CEO says private credit sector still viewed as attractive
  • Blue Owl private credit fund raises $400M via bond market
  • Ares Management plans ~$20B private credit fund - report
  • JPMorgan call seen focusing on trading, private credit as M&A expectations fade
  • Investors asked to withdraw over $20B from private credit funds in Q1, FT says

data.title: India to set up local engine repair facility for Tejas jets under GE Aerospace deal
data.description:
GE Aerospace (GE) reached an agreement with the Indian Air Force to help establish a domestic facility to maintain engines used in India’s Tejas fighter jets, a move aimed at reducing reliance on overseas repairs.

The new site will support the F404-IN20 engines that power the Tejas fleet. It will be owned and operated by the Indian Air Force, while GE Aerospace (GE) will provide technical assistance, training and parts.

Once operational, the facility is expected to shorten repair times by allowing work to be carried out in India rather than sending engines abroad. Faster turnaround could help improve aircraft availability for the air force.

The agreement builds on a long-standing relationship between GE Aerospace (GE) and India’s military, which spans several decades. In addition to the Tejas program, the company’s engines are used across a range of Indian platforms, including maritime patrol aircraft, helicopters and naval vessels.

The plan also reflects a broader push by India to expand its domestic defense capabilities and reduce dependence on foreign support for maintenance and logistics.

The timeline for completing the facility was not disclosed.

data.title: L3Harris tests autonomous electronic warfare system in U.S. Army exercise
data.description:
L3Harris Technologies (LHX) on Tuesday said it recently demonstrated an autonomous electronic warfare capability during a U.S. Army experiment, highlighting advances in systems designed to detect and disrupt threats with limited human input.

The test involved the company’s Deceptor electronic warfare payload, which was mounted on multiple unmanned aerial systems. Operating within a broader networked environment, the system was used to identify and track radio frequency signals, combine data from different sensors and carry out jamming operations against identified threats.

According to the company, the system is designed to operate across distributed platforms, enabling coordination between multiple unmanned assets in real time. The demonstration also included the ability to geolocate signals and share targeting data across the network.

Electronic warfare, which focuses on controlling and exploiting the electromagnetic spectrum, has become an increasing area of focus for military planners as modern conflicts rely more heavily on communications and sensor networks.

L3Harris (LHX) said the payload uses commercially available hardware and a modular design, allowing it to be integrated across a range of air, ground and maritime systems. The company indicated that its approach is aimed at enabling deployment across large numbers of smaller platforms rather than relying solely on traditional, centralized systems.

The demonstration builds on ongoing development of compact electronic warfare technologies, with additional tests planned to further integrate the system into multi-domain operations.

data.title: Pentagon awards Lockheed Martin up to $1.9B contract for C-130J training systems
data.description:
The U.S. Department of Defense awarded Lockheed Martin (LMT) a contract worth up to $1.9 billion to continue providing training systems and support for the C-130J military transport aircraft, according to a company release.

The agreement is structured as a 10-year, sole-source, indefinite delivery, indefinite quantity contract, allowing the Pentagon to order services as needed over the life of the program.

The contract covers the next phase of the C-130J Maintenance and Aircrew Training System program, which provides simulators, instructional materials and logistical support for pilots and maintenance personnel. Work will be carried out for multiple U.S. military branches, coordinated through the Air Force Life Cycle Management Center.

The program currently supports units across the Air Force, Air National Guard, Air Force Reserve, Marine Corps and Air Force Special Operations Command, among others. Under the new contract, training support will be expanded to include the Navy Reserve and the Coast Guard.

The C-130J is a modernized version of the long-running Hercules transport aircraft, widely used for tactical airlift missions. The platform is operated by dozens of countries, and the global fleet has accumulated millions of flight hours.

Indefinite delivery contracts such as this one are commonly used by the Pentagon for long-term service and support programs, enabling flexibility in procurement while maintaining continuity for established systems.
